content:
Ryan, it slowly trades a living world for a manageable machine.

“Windows of heaven” isn’t purple prose; it’s truthful, God-given speech that names reality as personal, covenantal, and God-governed. When a man reflexively converts that to “meteorological condensation event,” he may keep a fact and lose the frame. Over time, that costs him:

- Doxology. The Bible’s language trains the soul to worship. “Windows of heaven” points to the Lord who acts. “Condensation event” points to a process. If processes take center stage, praise drifts to the margins.
- Fear of the Lord. Scripture keeps God as first cause, with secondary causes under His hand. Reducing everything to mechanism forms a “closed-world” habit where God becomes an optional overlay. Reverent awe thins.
- Imagination and affections. God speaks in images to shape how we love and feel, not just what we know. Starving the biblical imagination makes us, in Lewis’s phrase, men without chests—sharp minds, weak hearts.
- Prayerful speech. You can’t easily pray the Psalms if you’ve trained yourself to translate them away. “He makes the clouds His chariot” becomes a figure to be explained rather than truth to be adored.
- Readiness to obey. A mechanized reading encourages mastery of the text, not submission to the Speaker. We start to handle Scripture instead of letting Scripture handle us.
- Communion with the saints. You drift from the Bible’s own cadence and from the church’s historic speech about God’s world, and that isolates you—subtly but surely.

None of this means science is bad or weather isn’t real. God delights to work through secondary causes. Call rain rain. Learn the physics. But keep the order straight: the Lord “opens the windows of heaven” (Genesis 7; Psalm 78), and the clouds obey. The cost comes when the clinical description displaces the confessional one.

A better habit is double vision under one Lord:
- Receive the Bible’s images as God’s authoritative way of forming your sight.
- Name the secondary causes without letting them evict the primary Cause.
- Let Scripture’s language tutor your tongue in prayer and your gut in wonder.

If we keep the biblical words, we keep the worship they carry. And in the end, that is what a man cannot afford to lose.
